does: This paper proposes FlexLLM, a moving target defense strategy that protects black-box LLMs against jailbreak attacks by dynamically altering decoding hyperparameters and system prompts at runtime. The defense works without requiring access to model internals or additional training, making it practical for API-based deployments. The core contribution involves two mechanisms: first, identifying and optimizing decoding hyperparameters that control token generation probabilities to improve robustness; second, continuously modifying these hyperparameters and system prompts as dynamic targets during inference. By randomizing the decoding strategy across requests, the approach raises the barrier for attackers to craft universally effective jailbreaks, since adversaries cannot reliably predict which configuration will be active when their prompt is processed.
connects: This work builds on two research streams: the emerging landscape of LLM jailbreak attacks and defenses, which has grown substantially as systems like ChatGPT become widely deployed, and the classical moving target defense paradigm from cybersecurity that randomizes system configurations to evade attacks. Recent jailbreak research has demonstrated vulnerabilities in prompt-based safety mechanisms, while defense literature has focused on retraining, fine-tuning, or architectural modifications. FlexLLM extends this by recognizing that API-based LLM deployment constraints—no model access, no retraining—require fundamentally different defenses. It combines insights from adversarial robustness in ML with parameter-space exploration to identify which decoding choices enhance resistance, while borrowing moving target concepts to make defenses adaptive and unpredictable rather than static.
enables: This approach opens several research directions. It enables further investigation into which decoding hyperparameters are most critical for safety, potentially informing better default configurations. It suggests a practical deployment framework for defense-in-depth strategies combining multiple randomization techniques. Future work could explore adaptive attacks that probe multiple decoding configurations, necessitating defenses that go beyond simple parameter randomization. The framework also enables studying the tradeoff between defense strength and output quality degradation. Finally, it points toward a new class of runtime-adaptive defenses for other safety properties in LLMs—such as reducing hallucinations or biases—through dynamic configuration, rather than relying solely on pretrained weights.
